video_title: Bolt-On: is Roose Bolton a skin-stealing immortal?
claim: Roose Bolton is a long-lived, skin-stealing being who periodically steals other people's identities by magically removing and wearing their skin, possibly a half-Other or even the Night's King himself.
sub_claims: Roose Bolton (or something wearing his skin) uses a form of blood magic, akin to the Faceless Men's face-changing, to periodically steal other people's skins/identities.
Roose Bolton is immortal or extremely long-lived and part-Other (possibly even the Night's King), tracing back to a Bolton ancestor's union with an Other described in one of Old Nan's stories.
Roose intends to eventually take Ramsay Bolton's skin and identity as his next host, which is why he tolerates and legitimizes Ramsay despite his psychotic, embarrassing behavior.
asx_verdict: The video calls Bolt-On plausible and fun to think about, but says there isn't any hugely compelling evidence either way -- the clues are 'vague hints' that could amount to nothing more than a red (or pale) herring.
notes: Single theory (no second distinct theory adjudicated). Short ~7-minute video, heavily narrator-driven speculation chained onto a handful of book quotes (Old Nan's story, Roose's face description, Roose's leeching line, Coldhands' corpse-decay description). One explicitly show-only aside (fireball-throwing child of the forest) flagged by the narrator as show material. Ends on an explicit low-confidence verdict.

paraphrase: The theory sounds crazy, but ASOIAF's setting already includes dragons, hundred-year-old psychic tree-people, ice zombies and stone zombies, mind-controlled giant wolves, necromancy, ten-year winters, a magic horn, accurate prophecies, resurrection, magical disguises, Faceless Men, and mammoth-riding giants attacking a 700-foot ice wall, so a skin-stealing immortal isn't obviously less plausible.
If the show is to be believed, the setting also includes fireball-throwing, skeleton-monster-fighting elf-children (children of the forest), further normalizing the weirdness of the theory.

The Faceless Men of Braavos definitively show it is possible to magically wear someone else's skin as an effective, wholly convincing whole-body disguise, via a blood-magic process that involves cutting one's own face.
The Boltons already flay their enemies and wear the skins as cloaks, showing an existing cultural practice adjacent to the skin-stealing magic the theory requires.

The Boltons have flayed people for centuries and are practically neighbours with Braavos, so it's plausible they could have discovered or learned this blood-and-skin magic themselves, the way Arya did from the Faceless Men.
ASOIAF has examples of immortal or at least very long-lived creatures, making it plausible that a character like Roose could also be one.
Roose Bolton is consistently described as pale and cold, and almost all of his appearances in the books mention his strange pale eyes, making him resemble an Other.
book_hint: Roose Bolton's appearances across POV chapters (unspecified)

paraphrase: One of Old Nan's stories tells of a man who loved a woman who is clearly an Other or something of the sort, described in specific terms.
spoken_quote: skin as white as the moon and eyes like blue stars

paraphrase: Old Nan says the man in the story may have been a Bolton, tying House Bolton directly to a union with an Other-like being.
The man in Old Nan's story gave something of himself -- his seed and, per the story, his soul -- to the Other-like woman, implying a child could have been born of the union.
Given the importance and power of blood in ASOIAF, a child of a man and an Other might have a longer life, strange or blue eyes, and a generally creepy presence, matching Roose's known traits.
Roose Bolton may be some kind of half-human, half-Other wearing human skin, possibly the Night's King himself.
A book description of Roose's face and eyes fits the theory perfectly on its face -- his skin as a mask, his eyes as ice.
book_hint: description of Roose Bolton (POV chapter unspecified)
spoken_quote: a pale grey mask, with two chips of dirty ice where his eyes should be

paraphrase: The 'pale grey mask' / 'chips of dirty ice' description could just be poetic language conveying that Roose's face and eyes are emotionless, give nothing away, and are harsh -- not literal evidence of an Other nature.

Roose implies he doesn't kill Ramsay for murdering Domeric only because that would be kinslaying, but the narrator questions whether a man who breaks guest right to betray and slaughter his allies would really care about kinslaying, suggesting Roose has an ulterior motive for keeping Ramsay around.
If Roose simply needed an heir, he and Walda could have had a child who is less of a PR liability than Ramsay, so his continued reliance on Ramsay needs another explanation.

The theory holds that Roose needs to change bodies periodically so people don't notice him aging past his 50s and 60s, and that he plans to take Ramsay's skin and identity once Ramsay is legitimized as heir of the Dreadfort and the North.

Ramsay's eyes are said to already look like Roose's, which is important: if Ramsay's eyes suddenly changed to Roose's distinctive wight-like eyes after a body swap, people would not become suspicious.
Alternatively, the Other-like eyes shared with Roose could indicate Ramsay himself has some degree of Other-ness, giving Roose another reason to want him around.

If Roose has an interest in pale-eyed Ramsay being his heir, maybe Roose himself killed Domeric rather than Ramsay -- poison hardly seems to be Ramsay's style, so Ramsay might be innocent of that murder.
Roose may have killed his other children as well for lacking his strange pale eyes.

Coldhands explains that once a person's heart stops beating, their blood runs down into the extremities and thickens and congeals there, which the theory uses to explain why Roose (if undead/Other-adjacent) would need to leech.
book_hint: Coldhands' dialogue (beyond the Wall, Bran's ASOS chapters)
spoken_quote: once the heart has ceased to beat, a man's blood runs down into his extremities, where it thickens and congeals

paraphrase: Coldhands further describes that this pooled, congealed blood causes the hands and feet to swell up and turn black, a visible symptom Roose could be using leeches to avoid.
book_hint: Coldhands' dialogue (beyond the Wall, Bran's ASOS chapters)
spoken_quote: his hands and feet swell up and turn as black as pudding

paraphrase: Roose himself explicitly links frequent leeching to long life, directly matching the theory's longevity claim.
spoken_quote: frequent leechings are the secret of a long life. A man must purge himself of bad blood

paraphrase: Objection: ASOIAF is about complex human beings doing wrong out of love, belonging, brokenness, and psychosis, not good guys fighting evil monsters, so explaining Roose's evil by making him an inhuman monster could cheapen that theme.
Rebuttal to the thematic objection: the Others already exist in the story as supernatural, seemingly evil monsters, so Roose being another kind of Other would not be unprecedented.

Roose is cruel to his people, but that cruelty maintains a peaceful, orderly North, and the Red Wedding arguably ended a war that would have caused far more death and misery than occurred at the Twins, suggesting even a supernatural Roose could be morally complex rather than simply an evil monster.
spoken_quote: a peaceful land, a quiet people

paraphrase: There has never been a Roose Bolton POV chapter, so readers haven't had a chance to see things from his (or its) point of view, meaning the theory can't be dismissed on the grounds of an internal perspective we simply don't have.
